questions.specification:
Harbour Café currently uses a **just in case (JIC)** approach for key ingredients: it keeps a large storeroom inventory of bread rolls, salad items, and packaged meats to avoid running out during busy periods.

The owner is considering switching to a **just in time (JIT)** approach:
- deliveries of bread and salad would arrive **twice daily**
- packaged meats would be delivered **every morning**
- the café would keep only a small buffer stock for unexpected demand

Recent issues have raised concerns:
- the café has had **spoilage** of salad items when demand is low
- a local road closure sometimes delays deliveries
- storage space is limited, and the storeroom could be converted into seating

A summary of the café’s current weekly figures is shown below.

| Item | Average units used per week | Cost per unit | Shelf life once delivered | Weekly waste under current system |
|---|---:|---:|---|---:|
| Bread rolls | 700 | $0.30 | 2 days | 40 rolls |
| Salad packs | 220 | $1.10 | 3 days | 25 packs |
| Packaged meats | 160 | $2.40 | 7 days | 5 packs |

parts.content: Outline one reason why Harbour Café might prefer a JIC approach rather than a JIT approach for at least one of its ingredients.
parts.markscheme:
- JIC keeps higher inventory so the café can still operate if a delivery is late/cancelled (e.g., road closures) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- JIC reduces the risk of stockouts and lost sales when demand spikes unexpectedly (e.g., sudden good weather) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: List two advantages of using a JIT inventory system for Harbour Café.
parts.markscheme:
- Less waste/spoilage due to holding less perishable stock <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Lower storage costs / frees space (e.g., storeroom converted to seating) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Less cash tied up in inventory / improved cash flow <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Stock is fresher, potentially improving product quality <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>Award any two.</note>

parts.content: Outline one disadvantage of using a JIT inventory system for Harbour Café.
parts.markscheme:
- Greater vulnerability to supply disruption (late deliveries/road closures) leading to stockouts and lost sales <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increased reliance on supplier reliability and coordination (more frequent deliveries) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
Harbour Café’s owner must choose between JIT and JIC for its inventory management.

Explain why the owner might use a combination of JIT and JIC across different ingredients when making this decision.
parts.markscheme:
- JIT and JIC are different approaches: JIT minimises stock by timing deliveries close to use; JIC holds extra stock as a buffer <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Different ingredients have different shelf lives and waste costs, so perishable items may suit JIT to reduce spoilage <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Items with higher risk/cost of running out (or where delivery disruption is likely) may suit JIC to protect sales/operations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A hybrid approach balances trade-offs: lower holding costs/waste while maintaining service levels through buffer stock on selected items <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A manufacturer of high-performance racing car components operates in an industry where engineering specifications are updated monthly. Why would this company prioritize a just-in-time (JIT) approach over a traditional inventory strategy?

options.content: To reduce the financial risk associated with component obsolescence
To protect the assembly line from sudden disruptions in the supply chain
To leverage economies of scale through high-volume component orders
To minimize the need for precise communication with external suppliers

questions.specification:
Hearth Harbour is a small company that assembles premium meal-kit boxes for subscription customers. Each box contains recipe cards and pre-portioned ingredients (fresh produce plus shelf-stable items). Demand varies by day, and some ingredients have short shelf lives.

![A packing bench with meal-kit boxes, recipe cards, and labelled ingredient bins ready for assembly](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/f67cd576-9346-4cb4-a20f-0ed481085c61.png)

parts.content: Define just-in-time (JIT) inventory management and just-in-case (JIC) inventory management in the context of Hearth Harbour’s ingredients.
parts.markscheme:
- JIT: ingredients are reordered/received to arrive only when needed for packing customer boxes, keeping stock levels low/minimising inventory held <marks>1 mark</marks>
- JIC: extra ingredients are held as buffer/safety stock to protect against demand spikes or supply delays, resulting in higher inventory held <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using the information given, outline two operational conditions that would make a JIC approach more appropriate than JIT for some of Hearth Harbour’s ingredients.
parts.markscheme:
- Unpredictable/volatile demand (eg daily variation) so buffer stock reduces risk of stock-outs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Unreliable/variable supplier lead times or delivery disruptions, so holding safety stock protects production/packing continuity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Short shelf-life/high spoilage risk can require careful selection: for some items, frequent small deliveries are impractical due to minimum order quantities/transport constraints, pushing toward holding stock <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Critical items with no close substitutes (single-sourced/specialty ingredients) where a stock-out stops completion of boxes <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content:
Hearth Harbour is considering switching from mostly JIC purchasing to a JIT approach for the next 6 months.

Explain one advantage and one disadvantage of this change for the business.  [6]
parts.markscheme:
- Advantage explained: lower inventory holding costs (less storage space, refrigeration, insurance, handling) leading to reduced unit costs/improved cash flow <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Develops advantage in context: less capital tied up in ingredients so more cash available for marketing/seasonal hiring/new recipes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Further development/impact: reduced waste/spoilage for fresh items because stock is held for a shorter time, improving gross margin and customer satisfaction <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Disadvantage explained: higher risk of stock-outs if deliveries are late or demand is higher than forecast, causing incomplete boxes/delayed shipments <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Develops disadvantage in context: missed delivery windows can trigger refunds/credits, damage subscription retention, and increase admin/customer service costs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Further development/impact: greater dependence on supplier reliability and transport; may require investment in forecasting/supplier agreements which could raise coordination costs <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 6 marks</note>

questions.specification:
A procurement manager is evaluating two different inventory models as summarized in the table below.

| Operational Metric | Manufacturer A | Manufacturer B |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Stock Level** | Buffer stock maintained for all parts | Parts arrive hours before assembly |
| **Warehouse Needs** | Large-scale on-site facilities | Minimal storage footprint |
| **Reliability Source** | Stockpiles of diverse components | Integrated, real-time supplier links |

![Industrial Storage](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-b051877e-f601-4f89-8521-486d54705b56-1771929956947/453c608c-5ce2-4acf-8b7a-2e5c088b4c00.jpeg)

Which conclusion correctly identifies the strategy used by **Manufacturer A** and a specific drawback associated with it?

options.content: Manufacturer A utilizes Just-in-time (JIT); a drawback is the high cost of maintaining large warehouse spaces.
Manufacturer A utilizes Just-in-case (JIC); a drawback is that significant capital is tied up in unsold inventory.
Manufacturer A utilizes Just-in-time (JIT); a drawback is the high risk of production stoppages due to supply chain delays.
Manufacturer A utilizes Just-in-case (JIC); a drawback is the inability to respond quickly to sudden spikes in consumer demand.

questions.specification: A textile manufacturer maintains large warehouses filled with surplus fabric to ensure they can meet any sudden increase in seasonal orders. What is a specific drawback of this 'Just in Case' (JIC) approach?

options.content: Increased dependency on the punctuality of transport providers for daily deliveries
Reduced ability to utilize economies of scale for raw material procurement
High costs associated with the storage and insurance of excess inventory
Inability to respond quickly to high-volume customer requests during peak periods

questions.specification:
ByteBento is a fast-growing meal-kit company that assembles chilled lunch boxes in a small regional factory and delivers them to offices each morning.

Some ingredients (e.g. cooked rice portions and sauce sachets) are supplied locally with a 24-hour lead time. Others (e.g. imported seaweed, specialty spice mixes) have a 3–4 week lead time and are occasionally delayed at ports.

ByteBento is deciding between two inventory approaches:

- **Option 1: Just in time (JIT)** — keep minimal stock, receive ingredients in smaller, frequent deliveries aligned to daily production.
- **Option 2: Just in case (JIC)** — keep higher safety stock of key ingredients to protect against late deliveries and demand spikes.

Recent events have affected the business:

- A competitor ran a promotion that caused sudden demand spikes on some days.
- Two deliveries of imported ingredients arrived one week late last month.
- The factory has limited chilled storage space and pays high electricity costs for refrigeration.
- Several suppliers charge extra for small, frequent deliveries.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/0cf57176-3fb1-4398-9555-124d7fd63942)

Figure 1: ByteBento’s ingredient packing line

![A dashboard-style graphic showing daily orders, supplier lead times, and a warning icon for delayed deliveries, alongside a small table of storage capacity](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/59b89ff0-9da1-4c32-8fa1-e191ea21b412.png)

Figure 2: ByteBento’s operations dashboard

parts.content: State what is meant by **just in time (JIT)** and **just in case (JIC)** stock control.
parts.markscheme:
- JIT: ordering/receiving inputs only when needed for production/dispatch, with minimal buffer stock <marks>1 mark</marks>
- JIC: holding extra stock (safety inventory) to cover uncertainty such as delays or unexpected demand <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Identify two features in the case study that suggest ByteBento may be vulnerable if it uses a pure JIT approach.
parts.markscheme:
- Long and/or variable lead times for imported ingredients (3–4 weeks) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Evidence of recent late deliveries (one week late) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sudden demand spikes due to competitor promotions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Limited chilled storage does not reduce vulnerability but increases pressure to run lean; vulnerability remains if any delay occurs <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Outline two advantages of adopting a JIT approach for ByteBento.
parts.markscheme:
- Lower holding costs (less chilled storage/electricity spend) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduced waste/spoilage of perishable ingredients due to fresher stock and less over-ordering <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Less working capital tied up in inventory, improving cash flow <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Less space required for storage, helping within limited capacity <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Explain how using a JIC approach for selected ingredients could improve ByteBento’s ability to meet customer orders.
parts.markscheme:
- Holding safety stock buffers against supplier delays, preventing stockouts and missed deliveries <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps maintain production continuity when lead times are long/uncertain (e.g. imported items) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allows the business to respond to short-notice demand spikes without immediate replenishment <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Can protect service levels/brand reputation by reducing cancelled orders <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explanation links the buffer stock to meeting daily dispatch deadlines <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss the likely advantages and disadvantages for ByteBento of operating a **hybrid system** (JIT for local ingredients and JIC for imported ingredients).
parts.markscheme:
- Applies JIT to short lead-time local items to minimise holding cost and spoilage <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Applies JIC to long lead-time/imported items to reduce risk of stockouts from delays <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Improves overall resilience/service level while keeping total inventory lower than full JIC <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Disadvantage: complexity of managing two systems (planning, reorder points, forecasting) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Disadvantage: higher capital tied up for imported safety stock and risk of obsolescence/spoilage (even if non-perishable, packaging changes/menu changes can make stock unusable) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Disadvantage: storage capacity constraints may still be exceeded for chilled items if JIC is applied too widely <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Disadvantage: supplier delivery charges for frequent small shipments can increase unit costs under JIT <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Balanced judgement with context (e.g. which approach best fits perishable vs imported items) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Coherent conclusion/decision justified using case evidence <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 9 marks</note>

topic.title: 10.1 Just in time (JIT) and just in case (JIC)
